papers.title: Variability of Hypervelocity Stars
papers.author_names: Idan Ginsburg Warren R. Brown Gary A. Wegner

abstract.text: We present time-series photometry of 11 hypervelocity stars (HVSs) to constrain their nature. Known HVSs are mostly late-B spectral type objects that may be either main-sequence (MS) or evolved blue horizontal branch (BHB) stars. Fortunately, MS stars at these effective temperatures, $T_{eff} \\\\sim$ 12,000 K, are good candidates for being a class of variable stars known as slowly pulsating B stars (SPBs). We obtained photometry on four nights at the WIYN 3.5 m telescope, and on six nights on the 2.4 m Hiltner telescope. Using sinusoidal fits, we constrain four of our targets to have periods between $P \\\\sim 0.2 - 2$ days, with a mean value of 0.6 days. Our amplitudes vary between $A = 0.5 - 3$%. This suggests that these four HVSs are SPBs. We discuss a possible origin for these stars, and why further observations are necessary.

brief.tldr: [research paper] Time-series photometry of 11 hypervelocity stars (HVSs) reveals pulsational variability in four targets with periods between 0.2–2 days (mean 0.6 days) and amplitudes of 0.5–3%, consistent with slowly pulsating B (SPB) star behavior driven by g-mode pulsations via the κ-mechanism. This supports the hypothesis that some HVSs are main-sequence B stars rather than evolved blue horizontal branch stars, with implications for their formation mechanisms and distance/age estimates, though further observations are needed to confirm the nature of fainter HVSs.

source_fields.sections_text: 3. Results: Three of the 11 hypervelocity stars (HVSs) show significant periodic variability consistent with stellar pulsations (SPBs) with periods between 3 and 120 hours, as confirmed by χ² and F-tests, with HVS1 exhibiting a detected amplitude of 0.0288 ± 0.0016 mag, while others show ambiguous or possible variability. 1. Introduction: This paper presents the first time-series photometric observations of 11 HVSs, addressing the problem of distinguishing between main-sequence and evolved binary star populations to improve distance, age, and mass estimates—critical for testing Hill’s binary disruption mechanism and understanding the nature of HVSs. 2. Observations: This section describes photometric observations of hypervelocity stars using the WIYN and MDM telescopes, with the primary goal of detecting variability, and finds that several stars, particularly HVS1, HVS4, HVS5, HVS7, and HVS13, show significant variability with high signal-to-noise ratios in WIYN data, while MDM data, though less precise, provide consistency checks. 4. Summary and Conclusion: This section concludes that four of the eleven hypervelocity stars (HVSs) exhibit variability consistent with subgiant or main-sequence B-star pulsations, supporting the hypothesis that many HVSs originate from the disruption of binary systems via Hill's mechanism, with a significant fraction likely being young, massive B stars.

abstract: We present time-series photometry of 11 hypervelocity stars (HVSs) to constrain their nature.
Known HVSs are mostly late-B spectral type objects that may be either main-sequence (MS) or evolved blue horizontal branch (BHB) stars.
Fortunately, MS stars at these effective temperatures, $T_{eff} \\\\sim$ 12,000 K, are good candidates for being a class of variable stars known as slowly pulsating B stars (SPBs).
We discuss a possible origin for these stars, and why further observations are necessary.
title: Variability of Hypervelocity Stars

catalog_assessment.evidence: Paper presents time-series photometry of 11 specific hypervelocity stars, analyzing their variability with measured periods and amplitudes.

papers.title: High Galactic latitude runaway stars as tracers of the spiral arms
papers.author_names: M. D. V. Silva R. Napiwotzki

abstract.text: A direct observation of the spiral structure of the Galaxy is hindered by our position in the middle of the Galactic plane. We propose a method based on the analysis of the birthplaces of high Galactic latitude runaway stars to map the spiral arms and determine their dynamics. As a proof of concept, the method is applied to a local sample of early-type stars and a sample of runaways stars to obtain estimates of the pattern speed and the spiral arm's phase angle. We also estimate the performance of this method once the data gathered by Gaia, in particular for runaway stars observed on the other side of the Galaxy, is available.

brief.tldr: [research paper] The paper proposes using high Galactic latitude runaway stars as tracers to map spiral arms and determine their pattern speed by reconstructing their birthplaces via orbital integration in the Galaxy's gravitational potential. A proof-of-concept analysis of local early-type stars and a sample of 96 runaway stars yields pattern speeds of 20.3±0.5 and 21.9±8.6 km s⁻¹ kpc⁻¹, and phase angles of −53.1°±3.1° and −63.5°±22.5°, respectively, with Gaia data expected to significantly improve accuracy by enabling large-scale, high-precision astrometry of runaway stars across the Galaxy.

source_fields.title: High Galactic latitude runaway stars as tracers of the spiral arms

source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Conclusions: This study demonstrates that tracing early-type stars and runaway stars—especially with Gaia data—can effectively reveal the positions and dynamics of spiral arms, including distant, previously obscured regions, with high accuracy and sufficient statistical power. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel method to trace the spiral arms of the Milky Way using the birthplaces of high Galactic latitude runaway stars, leveraging Gaia's high-precision astrometry to overcome the limitations of dust obscuration and interstellar reddening that hinder traditional tracers like H II regions or open clusters. Prospects with Gaia: The Gaia mission will significantly improve the accuracy of runaway star birthplace determinations, enabling precise birthplace coordinates for nearby and moderately distant stars, with uncertainties comparable to or smaller than spiral arm widths for stars brighter than V=13 and V=14. Results and discussion: The analysis of high Galactic latitude runaway stars reveals a unique solution for the spiral arm pattern speed, constrained primarily by intermediate-age stars (around 60 Myr), with younger stars providing better phase angle constraints but poor pattern speed determination and older stars leading to degeneracy due to insufficient travel time. Pattern speed determination: This section describes the selection and characterization of two stellar samples—runaway stars and local early-type main sequence stars—used to determine spiral arm properties and pattern speed, with the latter providing more precise birthplace determinations due to better distance measurements.

abstract: We propose a method based on the analysis of the birthplaces of high Galactic latitude runaway stars to map the spiral arms and determine their dynamics.
As a proof of concept, the method is applied to a local sample of early-type stars and a sample of runaways stars to obtain estimates of the pattern speed and the spiral arm's phase angle.
We also estimate the performance of this method once the data gathered by Gaia, in particular for runaway stars observed on the other side of the Galaxy, is available.
title: High Galactic latitude runaway stars as tracers of the spiral arms

catalog_assessment.evidence: Analyzes a sample of 96 runaway stars with orbital integration to trace spiral arms, implying use of existing observational data with astrometry/kinematics.
